data.title: IRS could lose billions if undocumented migrants stop filing taxes: study
data.description:
The Budget Lab at Yale estimated that the Internal Revenue Service could lose out on $313B over 10 years if migrants stop filing taxes and/or only take jobs where taxes wouldn't be withheld.

On Tuesday, the IRS agreed to share immigrants' tax data with Immigration and Customs Enforcement in ICE's aim to deport people who entered the U.S. illegally, according to media reports.

The Budget Lab estimated that unauthorized immigrants paid $66B in federal income and payroll taxes in 2023, about two-thirds of which as payroll taxes.

The effects of the IRS/ICE agreement could lead to a 0.5% loss in federal income and payroll tax revenue on average, or $25B in 2026, with the central range being $12B-$39B, the study said. That would come to $313B over 2026-'35 (central range of $147B-$479B).

"However, there is considerable uncertainty around this estimate, as it is hard to gauge how taxpayer behavior will adjust and the extent to which adjustment will be feasible," the report said.

In the case of immigrants failing to file taxes, the IRS could actually be getting increased revenue in cases where the taxpayer would be due a refund. However, if undocumented immigrants only take "under-the-table" jobs, where payroll taxes aren't withheld, then the IRS loses out on revenue.

"Relatedly but separately, undocumented immigrants may also become self-employed in increasing numbers, an arrangement that does not involve ongoing withholding," the report said.

The crucial question is whether unauthorized migrants and employers change their behavior at all.

"If none do whatsoever, then the effect on federal revenues of the agreement will be near zero (other than the fall in revenues resulting from any increased deportation operations, a distinct marginal effect not estimated here)," the Budget Lab said.  "If all do, the effect could be considerable."

data.title: Artisan Partners Asset Management reports March AUM of $162.39B
data.description:
• Artisan Partners Asset Management (NYSE:APAM) announced on Wednesday preliminary AUM of $162.39B as of March 31.
  • Artisan Funds and Artisan Global Funds accounted for $79.2B of the total AUM, while separate accounts and other AUM was $83.2B.

data.title: A wild, historic day for markets sees S&P 500 notch best day since 2008 financial crisis
data.description:
It was nothing short of a historic day for markets, as U.S. President Donald Trump's pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China sent Wall Street to its best day since the financial crisis of 2008. 

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) rocketed +9.52% to end at 5,456.90 points, its best intraday gain since October 2008. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) exploded +12.16% to settle at 17,124.97 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) surged +7.87% to conclude at 40,608.45 points.   

Trump said he would issue a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries who had reached out to the U.S. for trade negotiations.

He also upped the tariff rate against China to 125%. The Asian nation had earlier in the day raised its tariff rate on imports from the U.S. to 84%, while calling the American trade barriers "a mistake on top of a mistake."

"Just as it appeared that markets were about to take another leg lower because of the trade war started by the U.S., and retaliatory tariffs by other countries, especially China, the picture changed," Daniel Jones, investing group leader of Crude Value Insights, told Seeking Alpha.

"The market views the general scaling back as a net positive and likely as evidence that the trade war issue can largely be resolved, excluding China, over the next three months," he said. 

"Personally, I sold very little during this downturn. This is because I am a long-term investor. However, as we approach that 90 day mark, if things don't look better, I likely will begin selling some of my stock or I will at least buy some put options," Jones added. 

Wall Street had been gripped by the volatility that had become the norm over this week before Trump's announcement.   

Over in the fixed-income markets, a bruising bond selloff eased up after the announcement. A strong $39B 10-year notes by the U.S. Treasury department also helped sentiment.     

The 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 7 basis points to 4.37%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 20 basis points to 3.94%.  

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: SA Asks: Will tariffs derail Jeep and Chrysler owner Stellantis?
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), which owns Chrysler, Dodge and Jeep, has had a rough ride the past few weeks, shutting down production at its plants in Canada and Mexico and laying off 900 American workers as it braced for steep new tariffs on vehicles and auto parts imported into the United States.

Although the Trump administration announced Wednesday that it intends to pause some new tariffs for 90 days, the 25% auto tariff does not appear to be one of them. In addition, Stellantis (NYSE:STLA), which is based in the Netherlands, could still be subject to a 10% universal tariff on all foreign goods. 

So, will the new tariffs derail Stellantis (STLA)? We asked Seeking Alpha analyst Sungarden Investment for its take on the situation.

Sungarden Investment: The tariffs affect Stellantis (STLA) in two ways. 

First, the tariffs increase the level of uncertainty, which is already high for the industry as a whole and especially for Stellantis (STLA). 

One example is that the gross profit margin in Stellantis' TTM was 13%. Although in line with the margins of peers General Motors (GM) and Ford (F), it is an already pressured margin for a company that faces intense competition from Chinese players and exposure to several regions and brands, which increases the complexity of execution, in addition to factors common to the industry such as sensitivity to the macroeconomic scenario. The increase in uncertainty is enough to increase the level of volatility and retraction of multiples. 

Second, the tariffs - although it is not known how they will materialize in the long term - will affect the fundamentals and increase the complexity of Stellantis' (STLA) operations. The most obvious is that 40% of the cars the company sells in the U.S. are imported. This already causes the price of cars to rise and decreases the company's competitiveness; if it tries to absorb most of this loss, there could be great pressure on the margin in the short term. And there are other cost pressures, such as increases in the price of components and efforts to try to mitigate these tariffs, including layoffs and production migration, which can generate a lot of pressure (higher CapEx, delays, etc.) until it stabilizes.

Despite this, I don't think it's enough to “derail” Stellantis (STLA). The company's net debt is under control, and geographic diversification together with some strong brands helps prospects. But it's worth mentioning that it's certainly a strong headwind and could substantially slow down the fundamentals and consequently affect the stock.

data.title: Capital Southwest increases corporate credit facility to $510M
data.description:
• Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC)  announced on Wednesday an increase to the total commitments under its senior secured credit facility. 
  • The corporate credit facility was increased under the existing accordion feature by $25M, bringing total commitments from $485M to $510M. The $25M increase was provided by two existing lenders in the lender group, which consists of 11 participants. 
  • The existing accordion feature under the corporate credit facility allows for maximum commitments of up to $750M.

data.title: North West Company GAAP EPS of C$0.85, revenue of C$674.89M
data.description:
• North West Company press release (OTCPK:NNWWF): Q4 GAAP EPS of C$0.85.
  • Revenue of C$674.89M (+4.9% Y/Y).
  • Adjusted net earnings, which excludes the impact of the after-tax share-based compensation costs and Next 100 one-time costs, increased C$5.1 million or 12.8% compared to last year due to earnings gains in both Canadian Operations and International Operations.

data.title: Lucid Diagnostics announces proposed public offering of common stock
data.description:
• Lucid Diagnostics (NASDAQ:LUCD) intends to offer shares of common stock through an underwritten public offering, the company said on Wednesday.
  • The company also plans to grant the underwriters a 45-day option to purchase additional shares of common stock.
  • Proceeds from the offering are expected to support working capital and general corporate purposes. Completion of the offering remains subject to market conditions, with no assurance regarding its timing, size, or terms.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Glacier Bancorp receives regulatory approvals for Bank of Idaho acquisition
data.description:
• Glacier Bancorp (NYSE:GBCI) announced on Wednesday the receipt of regulatory approvals for the proposed acquisition of Bank of Idaho Holding (OTCQX:BOID).
  • The transaction, subject to BOID shareholders approval at the April 21 special meeting, is scheduled to close on April 30.

data.title: Tesla jumps 22% as it headlines a big day of tariff relief in the beat-up EV sector
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) led the electric vehicle sector higher on Wednesday after President Donald Trump dropped new tariff rates on imports from most U.S. trade partners to 10% for 90 days to set up more time for trade negotiations.

While Trump said imports from China will be hit with tariffs of 125% effective immediately, investors seem to be assuming that the U.S. and China will start to negotiate seriously. The risk of a trade war between the U.S. and China is a major consideration in the EV industry due to the reliance on China-made components and supply chain implications. 

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) was up 21.7% at 3:50 p.m. to put shares back to where they stood on April 3. Trading volume on Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) was over 173 million shares as the afternoon tariff announcement sent jolts across trading desks.

Other notable gainers in the EV sector included Polestar Automotive (PSNY) +18%, Lucid Group (LCID) +10.2%, Rivian Automotive (RIVN) +8.8%, VinFast Auto (VFS) +8.1%, Lotus Technology (LOT) +7.6%, Li Auto (LI) +7.1%, XPeng (XPEV) +6.5%, NIO (NIO) +5.8%, and ZEEKR Intelligent (ZK) +4.4%. Similar to Tesla (TSLA), most of those names were recouping losses built up over the last week and are still in negative territory for the year.

EV-related suppliers Stoneridge (SRI) +19.0%, REE Automotive (REE) +16.9%, QuantumScape (QS) +16.6%, and Hyliion Holdings (HYLN) +11.7% were also significant advancers off the tariff developments.

data.title: A US-built iPhone could cost 90% more, BofA says
data.description:
Following yesterday's comments from the White House that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) could build an iPhone in the U.S., Bank of America said an endeavor is “technically possible,” but it would come at an enormous cost.

Shares soared 15% on Wednesday, after President Trump said he would pause most tariffs for 90 days.

“In our opinion, iPhone cost can increase 25% purely on higher labor cost in the U.S.,” analyst Wamsi Mohan wrote in a note to clients. “On top of that, if Apple had to pay reciprocal tariffs to import sub-assemblies into the U.S., we see the total cost of an iPhone increasing 90%+.”

Mohan, who has a Buy rating and $250 price target on Apple, took his estimates based on the iPhone 16 Pro Max.

Assuming Apple could find the labor in the U.S. to assemble iPhones, Mohan said that a “significant” portion of the sub-assemblies would still need to be manufactured elsewhere, assembled in China and then imported to the U.S. “This is because, while it may be possible to move final assembly to the U.S., moving the entire iPhone supply chain would be a much bigger undertaking and would likely take many years, if even possible,” Mohan explained.

Despite efforts to diversify its supply chain geographically, Apple still relies on China for a significant portion of its manufacturing needs, specifically iPhones. Investment firm Needham recently estimated that Apple's fiscal 2025 earnings could be negatively impacted by 28% or more in a trade war between China and the U.S. unless it was given an exemption.

Aside from China, Apple and its partners manufacture iPhones, AirPods, Apple Watches, Macs and iPads in several countries, including India, Vietnam and Brazil. All of these countries have also been hit with tariffs by President Trump.

Apple releases its second quarter fiscal 2025 financial results on May 1. A consensus estimate expects Apple to report earnings per share of $1.61 on revenue of $94.04B.
